text: The hazard ratio for discontinuation with olanzapine versus quetiapine was 0.63 (95% CI, 0.52 to 0.76; P<0.001).
Remission rates did not differ significantly between venlafaxine and sertraline (odds ratio, 1.00; 95% CI, 0.66 to 1.51).
The hazard ratio for recovery with adjunctive antidepressant therapy was 1.01 (95% CI, 0.69 to 1.47; P=0.96).
The hazard ratio for new intervention for mood episode was 0.59 (95% CI 0.42-0.83; p=0.003) with lithium plus valproate versus valproate alone.
The mean change from baseline in CDR-SB at 18 months was -1.21 with lecanemab versus -1.66 with placebo (difference, -0.45; 95% CI, -0.67 to -0.23; P<0.001).
The difference in iADRS score change was 3.25 (95% CI, 1.88-4.62; P<0.001) favoring donanemab.
The difference in CDR-SB was -0.39 (95% CI, -0.69 to -0.09; P=0.01) favoring high-dose aducanumab.
The hazard ratio for confirmed disability progression was 0.78 (95% CI 0.64-0.96; p=0.021) with natalizumab.
The annualized relapse rate was 0.156 with ocrelizumab versus 0.292 with interferon (rate ratio, 0.54; 95% CI, 0.40 to 0.72; P<0.001).
The hazard ratio for confirmed disability progression was 0.76 (95% CI, 0.59 to 0.98; P=0.03) with ocrelizumab.
The hazard ratio for confirmed disability progression was 0.79 (95% CI 0.65-0.95; p=0.013) with siponimod.
The hazard ratio for relapse was 0.51 (95% CI, 0.40 to 0.66; P<0.001) with dimethyl fumarate.
The hazard ratio for disability progression was 0.58 (95% CI, 0.43 to 0.77; P<0.001) with natalizumab.
The annualized relapse rate was reduced by 54% with fingolimod versus placebo (rate ratio, 0.46; 95% CI, 0.36 to 0.59; P<0.001).
The hazard ratio for disability progression was 0.66 (95% CI 0.44-0.99; p=0.043) with ibudilast versus placebo.
